content:
Hi,

I want to learn rust for linux, and I'm currently looking for an article or guide on how to create a minimal VM environment using the kernel. Normally I just use debian and QEMU for this, but I want to make the environment more smaller without using a distro. How do you test the custom kernel, and is there any documentation on this? Thanks!
You can create a busybox VM as elaborated here
Maybe you'll also find this setup useful if you ignore the PuzzleFS specifics

Ariel Miculas said:

Maybe you'll also find this setup useful if you ignore the PuzzleFS specifics

I tried following the instruction, but qemu-busybox-min.config is missing from the rust-next branch. Is this config removed from the branch?

I also have a problem when building the kernel using this command:

make ARCH=x86_64 LLVM=1 -j$(nproc)

clang gives me the following error:

error: argument unused during compilation: '-fno-strict-overflow' [-Werror,-Wunused-command-line-argument]

and to fix this, I need to edit the Makefile and remove the -fno-strict-overflow flag. Why is this happening and how to fix this?
I personally found this tool really helpful https://github.com/arighi/virtme-ng

Pasha Fistanto said:

I tried following the instruction, but qemu-busybox-min.config is missing from the rust-next branch. Is this config removed from the branch?

Yes, the config isn't present in rust-next. You can find it here

Pasha Fistanto said:

I also have a problem when building the kernel using this command:

make ARCH=x86_64 LLVM=1 -j$(nproc)

clang gives me the following error:

error: argument unused during compilation: '-fno-strict-overflow' [-Werror,-Wunused-command-line-argument]

and to fix this, I need to edit the Makefile and remove the -fno-strict-overflow flag. Why is this happening and how to fix this?

Hmm, there were some discussions about this issue here:
#Help > build error
Are you using Nixos by any chance?

Ariel Miculas said:

Yes, the config isn't present in rust-next. You can find it here

Is there any reason for the deletion of the config?

Ariel Miculas said:

Are you using Nixos by any chance?

Thanks for the link! I'm also using NixOS for the development.
about the config, it's not that it was deleted, it's that it was never added
You can read here about the old rust branch: https://rust-for-linux.com/branches#rust
@Ariel Miculas Did you also have a problem when trying to compile busybox? I assume that I have some dependencies missing from my flake.. Could you also share your nix flake?
I've used nixos independently of my kernel VM setup, so I don't have a working flake. But share your errors here and I'll try to help.
See also this nixos discussion: #Help > ✔ Nixos development environment for Rust-for-Linux

I'm getting this problem when trying to compile busybox as a static binary:

❯ make
  LINK    busybox_unstripped
Your linker does not support --sort-section,alignment
Your linker does not support --sort-common
Static linking against glibc, can't use --gc-sections
Trying libraries: m resolv
Failed: -Wl,--start-group -lm -lresolv   -Wl,--end-group
Output of:
gcc -malign-data=abi -Wall -Wshadow -Wwrite-strings -Wundef -Wstrict-prototypes -Wunused -Wunused-parameter -Wunused-function -Wunused-value -Wmissing-prototypes -Wmissing-declarations -Wno-format-security -Wdeclaration-after-statement -Wold-style-definition -finline-limit=0 -fno-builtin-strlen -fomit-frame-pointer -ffunction-sections -fdata-sections -fno-guess-branch-probability -funsigned-char -static-libgcc -falign-functions=1 -falign-jumps=1 -falign-labels=1 -falign-loops=1 -fno-unwind-tables -fno-asynchronous-unwind-tables -fno-builtin-printf -Oz -static -o busybox_unstripped -Wl,--start-group applets/built-in.o archival/lib.a archival/libarchive/lib.a console-tools/lib.a coreutils/lib.a coreutils/libcoreutils/lib.a debianutils/lib.a klibc-utils/lib.a e2fsprogs/lib.a editors/lib.a findutils/lib.a init/lib.a libbb/lib.a libpwdgrp/lib.a loginutils/lib.a mailutils/lib.a miscutils/lib.a modutils/lib.a networking/lib.a networking/libiproute/lib.a networking/udhcp/lib.a printutils/lib.a procps/lib.a runit/lib.a selinux/lib.a shell/lib.a sysklogd/lib.a util-linux/lib.a util-linux/volume_id/lib.a archival/built-in.o archival/libarchive/built-in.o console-tools/built-in.o coreutils/built-in.o coreutils/libcoreutils/built-in.o debianutils/built-in.o klibc-utils/built-in.o e2fsprogs/built-in.o editors/built-in.o findutils/built-in.o init/built-in.o libbb/built-in.o libpwdgrp/built-in.o loginutils/built-in.o mailutils/built-in.o miscutils/built-in.o modutils/built-in.o networking/built-in.o networking/libiproute/built-in.o networking/udhcp/built-in.o printutils/built-in.o procps/built-in.o runit/built-in.o selinux/built-in.o shell/built-in.o sysklogd/built-in.o util-linux/built-in.o util-linux/volume_id/built-in.o -Wl,--end-group -Wl,--start-group -lm -lresolv -Wl,--end-group
==========
/nix/store/81xsp348yfgmaan9r5055mcdjfw7a8wc-binutils-2.42/bin/ld: cannot find -lm: No such file or directory
/nix/store/81xsp348yfgmaan9r5055mcdjfw7a8wc-binutils-2.42/bin/ld: cannot find -lresolv: No such file or directory

what could be the culprit here? I tried adding glibc as a dependency but it didn't work..
I'm looking at the busybox nix pkg, I see it uses $out as CONFIG_PREFIX, but I'm not sure how you would get the RFS from there. I need to learn more nix.

I guess you need to use pkgsStatic.busybox, figure out where the rootfs gets copied, then go from there. I can see that pkgStatic.busybox installed the following on my system:

$ ls -la /nix/store/hcdjs6yjqmw5a5y4gilrlvq855m7igyv-busybox-static-x86_64-unknown-linux-musl-1.36.1/
total 784
dr-xr-xr-x    3 root     root          4096 Jan  1  1970 .
drwxrwxr-t 1151 root     nixbld      778240 Oct  7 20:37 ..
dr-xr-xr-x    2 root     root         12288 Jan  1  1970 bin
-r-xr-xr-x    1 root     root          2274 Jan  1  1970 default.script
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root            11 Jan  1  1970 linuxrc -> bin/busybox
lrwxrwxrwx    1 root     root             3 Jan  1  1970 sbin -> bin

where the bin has all the busybox binaries. So if you can get this path, you can add the init script and then create your rootfs.
Hmm that means compiling the busybox manually is not recommended on nixos?

Luna said:

I personally found this tool really helpful https://github.com/arighi/virtme-ng

Indeed it seems the easiest option nowadays, @Andrea Righi talk about it some days ago: https://youtu.be/ZgMLGM2UazY?si=rMczznxa7AU-rwo7
Although I dont know if it supports NixOs.
Someone started working at the NixOs support for virtme-ng, see https://github.com/arighi/virtme-ng/issues/95 but I'm not sure if it's working...
If you use NixOS then it might be easier to just have use Nix to build a VM, e.g. nixos-rebuild build-vm
@Andreas Hindborg might have some config to share

Pasha Fistanto said:

Hmm that means compiling the busybox manually is not recommended on nixos?

Well I believe you would do it like this: https://gist.github.com/ariel-miculas/41d5e62d94f9927e4c21e6dc1019277f
Then run the following steps:

$ nix develop --extra-experimental-features nix-command --extra-experimental-features flakes
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:~/work/kernel-devel$ which busybox
/nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1/bin/busybox
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:~/work/kernel-devel$ cd /nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1/
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:/nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1$ find . | cpio -H newc -o | gzip > ~/work/kernel-devel/ramdisk.img
5308 blocks
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:/nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1$ cd -
/home/amiculas/work/kernel-devel
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:~/work/kernel-devel$ qemu-system-x86_64 -accel kvm -cpu host -m 4G -initrd ramdisk.img -kernel ~/work/linux/arch/x86/boot/bzImage -nographic -append 'console=ttyS0 nokaslr debug rdinit=/init' -nic user,model=rtl8139 -no-reboot

Here I'm assuming you have already built the kernel with qemu-busybox-min.config and rust enabled.
But there are probably easier ways, as Gary mentioned.

I couldn't figure out why networking doesn't work so for now I've commented the line 

udhcpc -i eth0

from the init script.

Gary Guo said:

Andreas Hindborg might have some config to share

I have kernel config fragments that I use to boot NixOS on real HW. I can share if there is any interest.

Ariel Miculas said:

Well I believe you would do it like this: https://gist.github.com/ariel-miculas/41d5e62d94f9927e4c21e6dc1019277f
Then run the following steps:

$ nix develop --extra-experimental-features nix-command --extra-experimental-features flakes
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:~/work/kernel-devel$ which busybox
/nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1/bin/busybox
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:~/work/kernel-devel$ cd /nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1/
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:/nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1$ find . | cpio -H newc -o | gzip > ~/work/kernel-devel/ramdisk.img
5308 blocks
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:/nix/store/vawdvyxri5hx2mpgwf9iixdrcb4ggnsj-busybox-1.36.1$ cd -
/home/amiculas/work/kernel-devel
amiculas@amiculas-l-PF3FCGJH:~/work/kernel-devel$ qemu-system-x86_64 -accel kvm -cpu host -m 4G -initrd ramdisk.img -kernel ~/work/linux/arch/x86/boot/bzImage -nographic -append 'console=ttyS0 nokaslr debug rdinit=/init' -nic user,model=rtl8139 -no-reboot

Here I'm assuming you have already built the kernel with qemu-busybox-min.config and rust enabled.
But there are probably easier ways, as Gary mentioned.

I tried this solution, but busybox will replace my coreutils in my dev environment since it's added as buildInputs which I find very annoying..
well it must be a way to install it somewhere else where it doesn't affect the $PATH
I bet there's a way to do that, but I'm not that proficient yet with nix.. Anyways thank you very much for the help! For now I will just do it the simple way using debian image
You're welcome. Yeah, nix definitely requires time until you get the hang of it, I'm a beginner myself.

Andrea Righi said:

Someone started working at the NixOs support for virtme-ng, see https://github.com/arighi/virtme-ng/issues/95 but I'm not sure if it's working...

Interesting project! I'll try this tool on nixOS later
